coredata.dc:title: Ultraviolet encryption information storage using hydrophobic poly(ε-caprolactone)/carbon quantum dot composite film
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Yu
Hsu, Yu-I
Uyama, Hiroshi
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent poly(ε-caprolactone)/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (PCL/N-CQDs) film with the function of information protection and encryption was realized based on the fluorescent N-CQDs. The N-CQDs were synthesized from citric acid with urea as a dopant for the enhancement of fluorescent properties. The facilely prepared composite film retained the hydrophobic nature of PCL and achieved the enhanced mechanical property. The appropriate crystallization temperature and degree of crystallinity of PCL provide the film with opaque characteristics at room temperature, rendering it an ideal carrier for carrying information. The information encryption and decryption are achieved through ionoprinting technique, exploiting the ability of Fe3+ ions to quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs, allowing for the encryption of designed information on the film’s surface. With its opaque, hydrophobic, and photoluminescent properties, the PCL/N-CQDs composite film holds significant promise for applications in information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Composite
Film
Hydrophobic
Information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR (b) XPS survey, (c) UV–vis, and (d-f) Florescent spectra of N-CQDs.
(a) TEM images of N-CQDs0.8 from different regions. (b) Size distribution of N-CQDs0.8.
(a) Water contact angle and (b) tensile stress–strain curves of neat PCL film and PCL/N-CQD films. (c) Photos of the ionoprinting process of PCL/N-CQDs film under visible and UV light.

λ ex , λ em
, and QY value of N-CQDs synthesized from CA/urea.

head.ce:title: Ultraviolet encryption information storage using hydrophobic poly(ε-caprolactone)/carbon quantum dot composite film

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Graduate School of Engineering, Osaka University, Yamadaoka 2-1, Suita, Osaka 565-0871, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Graduate School of Engineering, Osaka University, Yamadaoka 2-1, Suita, Osaka 565-0871, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent poly(ε-caprolactone)/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (PCL/N-CQDs) film with the function of information protection and encryption was realized based on the fluorescent N-CQDs. The N-CQDs were synthesized from citric acid with urea as a dopant for the enhancement of fluorescent properties. The facilely prepared composite film retained the hydrophobic nature of PCL and achieved the enhanced mechanical property. The appropriate crystallization temperature and degree of crystallinity of PCL provide the film with opaque characteristics at room temperature, rendering it an ideal carrier for carrying information. The information encryption and decryption are achieved through ionoprinting technique, exploiting the ability of Fe3+ ions to quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs, allowing for the encryption of designed information on the film’s surface. With its opaque, hydrophobic, and photoluminescent properties, the PCL/N-CQDs composite film holds significant promise for applications in information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: With technology advancements, private information leakage often occurs. Information storage has been one of topics that are central to the knowledge of the 21st century [1]. Considerable research efforts have been devoted for safeguarding information involving fluorescent materials such as anticounterfeiting materials [2] and fluorescent ink [3]. However, conventional fluorescent materials are costly and toxic. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) offered a promising alternative due to cost-effective synthesis methods [4]. Composite materials incorporating CQDs hold potential for enhancing information encryption and storage capabilities.

While hydrogels containing CQDs have received attention for encryption [5], investigations into composite films remain limited. Films offer advantages like higher specific surface area and portability. Polymers are favored metals or inorganics for films due to their low cost and high processability. Biodegradable polymers show promise over non-biodegradable options which pose environmental concerns [6]. Among them, poly(ε-caprolactone) (PCL), with its suitable crystalline temperature and hydrophobic nature aiding self-cleaning, is ideal for practical applications.

In this study, a photoluminescent composite film incorporating PCL and nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) was prepared for information encryption. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibited an excitation-independent photoluminescence with a high quantum yield (QY) of 17 %. The composite film demonstrated excellent mechanical properties alongside controllable hydrophilicity. Leveraging the fluorescence of N-CQDs enabled successful information encryption and decryption, providing a novel approach to information protection, and mitigating the risk of information leakage.

N-CQDs were prepared via hydrothermal method based on a reported route [7]. 1 g of citric acid (CA) and x (x = 0.6, 0.8, 1.0) g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water in a Teflon autoclave (20 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 20 min to remove impurities. N-CQDs0.6, N-CQDs0.8, and N-CQDs1.0 were obtained after 3 days of freeze-drying, corresponding to different urea quantities.

PCL/N-CQDs films were prepared via solvent casting. PCL (1.5 g) was dissolved in 1,4-dioxane (19 mL) under stirring for 4 h. Subsequently, N-CQDs aqueous solution (1 mL) was added with ultrasonication. The resulting mixture was casted onto a Petri dish and air-dried at 30 °C. The films, designated as film 0, film 1, film 2, film 3 and film 4, correspond to N-CQDs content of 0 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.3 wt% and 0.4 wt% relative to PCL, respectively (Table S1).

The chemical compositions and size of N-CQDs were confirmed by ATR-FTIR, XPS, TEM and AFM. Optical and photoluminescence properties were investigated using a microplate reader. Hydrophilicity and mechanical of composite films were confirmed by water contact angle and tensile testing. Check the supplemental file for detailed information.

Surface loading of information onto PCL/N-CQDs was achieved via ionoprinting [8]. Filter paper was immersed in a FeCl3 solution (125 mM) for 2 h, cut into the desired shapes, and placed onto the film for 5 min. The loaded information was visible only under specific UV light.

FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1
(a)) displayed absorption peaks of CO or CN, COO−, C-N, and C-O at 1660 cm−1, 1550 cm−1, 1185 cm−1 and 1047 cm−1. Peaks in the range of 1412–1346 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C-N from aromatic amine groups. A broad peak around 3000 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H, confirming the good water solubility [9]. Comparative analysis showed increased prominence of the CN peak with higher C-N group content, indicating enhanced doping effects.

XPS analysis (Fig. 1 (b) and Table S2) revealed N concentrations exceeding 10 % in all N-CQDs, confirming successful nitrogen doping. N-CQDs0.8 exhibited the lowest C/O and C/N ratios, suggesting greater functional group presence. Further XPS analysis (Figure S1) supported successful nitrogen incorporation from urea into N-CQDs, evidenced by C-N and CN bonds.

TEM images (Fig. 2 ) revealed the spherical nature of N-CQDs nanoparticles with an average particle size of 21.5 nm. Additionally, the AFM images (Figure S2) revealed uniformly globular structures with a height of 7 nm, further confirming their nanoscale size.

EDS results (Table S4) revealed the presence of C, N, and O of pristine PCL and PCL/N-CQDs films. Notably, N exhibited an atomic ratio of approximately 15 %, with the highest concentration observed in film 2. Mapping images indicated the uniform dispersion of N-CQDs (Figure S3).

The hydrophilicity was investigated using water contact angle measurement (Fig. 3 (a)). A decrease in water contact angle was observed with higher of N-CQDs contents, transitioning to a hydrophilic state beyond 0.3 %. This transition was attributed to the exposure of N-CQDs on the film surface, increasing roughness and lowering surface energy [14]. As the N-CQDs content increased, the inherently hydrophilic nature of N-CQDs contributed to a corresponding decrease in the water contact angle.

Tensile tests (Fig. 3(b)) revealed the elastomeric properties of PCL and PCL/N-CQDs films (Figure S4). Film 2 demonstrated improved mechanical properties, with a tensile strength of 45.44 MPa and elongation at break of 1342.56 % (Table S3), compared to 32.39 MPa and 947.35 % for film 0. Therefore, film 2 was selected for further study.

It has been reported that the fluorescence of N-CQDs can be quenched by Fe3+, demonstrating the potential of the composite film for information encryption. As depicted in Fig. 3 (c), upon ionoprinting with Fe3+, a predefined pattern of the alphabet “U” became readily visible on the surface of the PCL/N-CQDs film under UV irradiation, while remaining undetectable under visible light. The fluorescent characteristics of N-CQDs serve to fulfill the function of information protection and encryption. Additionally, the opacity of the composite film enables the pattern to be easily discerned without requiring a background panel.

Photoluminescent N-CQDs were successfully synthesized from CA/urea via hydrothermal method, with a CA/urea ratio of 5:4 yielding higher C-N functional group contents and QY value. These N-CQDs0.8 exhibited an average diameter of 21.5 nm and excitation-independent photoluminescence. PCL/N-CQDs composite films were successfully fabricated via solvent casting, offering adjustable hydrophilicity by varying the N-CQDs content. Retaining the photoluminescent properties of N-CQDs, these films enable information encryption via ionoprinting. This opaque, hydrophobic, and photoluminescent composite film holds significant potential for information encryption applications.

Yu Cao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yu-I Hsu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hiroshi Uyama: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Japan Science and Technology Agency (JST) PRESTO Grant Number JPMJPR23N4, the Environment Research and Technology Development Fund JPMEERF21S11900 of the Environmental Restoration and Conservation Agency of Japan, and Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) KAKENHI Grants (22K21348 and 23K26717).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.137027.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

C 11 (2023) 16177–16185. https://doi.org/10.1039/D3TC03136E.
